annotations.label: Naive set theory

anchor.snippet: Naive set theory is any of several set theories used in the discussion of the foundations of mathematics

annotations.note: A meta-level historical/foundational label for informal set theories; not itself a Mathlib object (Mathlib.Data.Set.Defs provides the type-theoretic Set instead).

annotations.label: Naive theory (informal)

anchor.snippet: A naive theory in the sense of "naive set theory" is a non-formalized theory

annotations.note: A meta-mathematical description of non-formal theories, with no counterpart declaration in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Russell's paradox

anchor.snippet: One common example is Russell's paradox : there is no set consisting of "all sets that do not contain themselves"

annotations.note: The paradox's conclusion that no universal set exists is captured in ZFC by Class.univ_notMem_univ (verified) and ZFSet.mem_irrefl, but the paradoxical set itself is not formally constructed.

annotations.label: Cantor's paradox derivation

anchor.snippet: Cantor's paradox can actually be derived from the above (false) assumption

annotations.note: Cantor's theorem a < 2^a underpinning the paradox is formalized as Cardinal.cantor (verified); the paradox as a naive-set derivation is not itself stated.

annotations.label: Gödel's incompleteness (consistency)

anchor.snippet: It follows from Gödel's incompleteness theorems that a sufficiently complicated first-order logic system

annotations.note: Gödel's incompleteness theorems are not present in Mathlib; only unrelated first-order model-theory infrastructure (e.g., FirstOrder.Language.Theory.IsComplete) exists.

annotations.label: Set (Cantor)

anchor.snippet: In naive set theory, a set is described as a well-defined collection of objects

annotations.note: Sets over a type are `Set α := α → Prop` in Mathlib.Data.Set.Defs (verified).

annotations.label: 4 is even

anchor.snippet: For instance, 4 is a member of the set of all even integers

annotations.note: A pedagogical example illustrating membership; not a stand-alone Mathlib fact (though Even 4 is trivially provable).

annotations.label: Membership

anchor.snippet: If x is a member of a set A , then it is also said that x belongs to A

annotations.note: Set membership is `Set.Mem` with the standard `Membership` instance in Mathlib.Data.Set.Defs (verified).

annotations.label: Equality of sets

anchor.snippet: Two sets A and B are defined to be equal when they have precisely the same elements

annotations.note: Set.ext in Mathlib.Data.Set.Defs proves two sets equal iff they share the same members (verified).

annotations.label: Primes less than 6

anchor.snippet: the set with elements 2, 3, and 5 is equal to the set of all prime numbers less than 6

annotations.note: A pedagogical example illustrating extensionality with no dedicated Mathlib lemma.

annotations.label: Empty set

anchor.snippet: is a set with no members at all

annotations.note: The empty set on `Set α` is provided by the `EmptyCollection` instance (`∅ = fun _ ↦ False`) in Mathlib.Data.Set.Defs (read directly).

annotations.label: Uniqueness of the empty set

anchor.snippet: Because a set is determined completely by its elements, there can be only one empty set

annotations.note: Set.eq_empty_of_forall_notMem shows any set with no members equals ∅ (verified via loogle), giving uniqueness of the empty set.

annotations.label: Extensional set notation

anchor.snippet: The simplest way to describe a set is to list its elements between curly braces

annotations.note: Roster notation `{a, b, ...}` is provided by the `Singleton` and `Insert` instances on `Set α` in Mathlib.Data.Set.Defs (read directly).

annotations.label: Set-builder notation

anchor.snippet: is used to denote the set containing all objects for which the condition P holds

annotations.note: `{x | p x}` desugars to `setOf p` with the elaborator defined in Mathlib.Data.Set.Defs (read directly).

annotations.label: Subset

anchor.snippet: A is a subset of B if every element of A is also an element of B

annotations.note: Set.Subset in Mathlib.Data.Set.Defs is defined as `∀ ⦃a⦄, a ∈ s₁ → a ∈ s₂` (read directly).

annotations.label: Proper subset

anchor.snippet: a subset of B that is not equal to B is called a proper subset

annotations.note: Proper subset `⊂` on `Set α` is provided through the `HasSSubset` instance (`a ⊂ b ↔ a ⊆ b ∧ ¬ b ⊆ a`) available on any preorder including `Set α`.

annotations.label: Superset

anchor.snippet: If A is a subset of B , then one can also say that B is a superset of A

annotations.note: `⊇` is the flip of `⊆` on `Set α`, available via the `HasSubset`/`LE` instances defined in Mathlib.Data.Set.Defs (read directly).

annotations.label: Equality via mutual inclusion

anchor.snippet: A = B if and only if A ⊆ B and B ⊆ A

annotations.note: Set.Subset.antisymm gives A = B from A ⊆ B and B ⊆ A (verified via decl_exists).

annotations.label: Empty set is subset of every set

anchor.snippet: The empty set is a subset of every set

annotations.note: Set.empty_subset proves `∅ ⊆ s` for any set (verified via decl_exists).

annotations.label: Power set

anchor.snippet: The set of all subsets of a given set A is called the power set of A

annotations.note: Set.powerset (`𝒫 s`) is `{t | t ⊆ s}` in Mathlib.Data.Set.Defs (read directly).

annotations.label: Cardinality of power set

anchor.snippet: If the set A has n elements, then

annotations.note: Set.ncard_powerset gives `|𝒫 s| = 2^|s|` for a finite set (verified via decl_exists).

annotations.label: Universal set

anchor.snippet: one may consider all sets under consideration as being subsets of some given universal set

annotations.note: Set.univ := `{_a | True}` is defined in Mathlib.Data.Set.Defs (read directly).

annotations.label: Absolute complement

anchor.snippet: the complement of A (in U ) is defined as

annotations.note: Set.compl := `{a | a ∉ s}` is defined in Mathlib.Data.Set.Defs (read directly).

annotations.label: Union

anchor.snippet: their union is the set consisting of all objects which are elements of A or of B or of both

annotations.note: Set.union := `{a | a ∈ s₁ ∨ a ∈ s₂}` is defined in Mathlib.Data.Set.Defs (read directly).

annotations.label: Intersection

anchor.snippet: The intersection of A and B is the set of all objects which are both in A and in B

annotations.note: Set.inter := `{a | a ∈ s₁ ∧ a ∈ s₂}` is defined in Mathlib.Data.Set.Defs (read directly).

annotations.label: Relative complement

anchor.snippet: the relative complement of B relative to A , also known as the set theoretic difference of A and B

annotations.note: Set.diff := `{a ∈ s | a ∉ t}` (i.e. `s \ t`) is defined in Mathlib.Data.Set.Defs (read directly).

annotations.label: Left-handed and blond people

anchor.snippet: let A be the set of left-handed people, and let B be the set of people with blond hair

annotations.note: Pedagogical illustration of union/intersection with no formal counterpart.

annotations.label: No human over 1000 years

anchor.snippet: Now let E be the set of all human beings, and let F be the set of all living things over 1000 years old

annotations.note: Pedagogical illustration with no Mathlib analogue.

annotations.label: Power set Boolean algebra

anchor.snippet: the power set [MATH] is a Boolean algebra under the operations of union and intersection

annotations.note: `Set α` carries a `BooleanAlgebra` instance under ∪, ∩, and complement in Mathlib's set order/lattice API.

annotations.label: Ordered pair

anchor.snippet: an ordered pair is simply a collection of two objects such that one can be distinguished as the first element and the other as the second element

annotations.note: Ordered pairs are `Prod α β` from Init.Prelude (verified via decl_exists).

annotations.label: Equality of ordered pairs

anchor.snippet: two ordered pairs ( a , b ) and ( c , d ) are equal if and only if a = c and b = d

annotations.note: Prod.mk.injEq: `((a,b) = (c,d)) = (a = c ∧ b = d)` (verified via loogle).

annotations.label: Cartesian product

anchor.snippet: If A and B are sets, then the Cartesian product (or simply product ) is defined to be:

annotations.note: Set.prod builds `s ×ˢ t = {p | p.1 ∈ s ∧ p.2 ∈ t}` in Mathlib.Data.Set.Operations (verified via decl_exists).

annotations.label: Natural numbers

anchor.snippet: Natural numbers are used for counting

annotations.note: Nat is the core natural-number type (verified via decl_exists).

annotations.label: Integers

anchor.snippet: Integers appear as solutions for x in equations like x + a = b

annotations.note: Int is the core integer inductive type (verified via decl_exists).

annotations.label: Rational numbers

anchor.snippet: Rational numbers appear as solutions to equations like a + bx = c

annotations.note: Rat is the core rationals type (verified via decl_exists).

annotations.label: Algebraic numbers

anchor.snippet: Algebraic numbers appear as solutions to polynomial equations (with integer coefficients)

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes `IsAlgebraic` and `AlgebraicClosure ℚ`, but there is no distinguished type `𝔸` of algebraic numbers as a named subfield of ℂ (verified via decl_exists).

annotations.label: Real numbers

anchor.snippet: Real numbers represent the "real line" and include all numbers that can be approximated by rationals

annotations.note: Real is Mathlib's Cauchy-completion of ℚ (verified via decl_exists).

annotations.label: Complex numbers

anchor.snippet: Complex numbers are sums of a real and an imaginary number

annotations.note: Complex is Mathlib's field of complex numbers (verified via decl_exists).

annotations.label: Axiom schema of unrestricted comprehension

anchor.snippet: The unrestricted formation principle of sets referred to as the axiom schema of unrestricted comprehension

annotations.note: Unrestricted comprehension is inconsistent, and Mathlib's foundations (Lean type theory) do not state it as an axiom schema.

annotations.label: Axiom schema of specification

anchor.snippet: If the axiom schema of unrestricted comprehension is weakened to the axiom schema of specification or axiom schema of separation

annotations.note: The separation/specification schema is realized as ZFSet.sep with `ZFSet.mem_sep` giving `y ∈ sep p x ↔ y ∈ x ∧ p y` (verified via decl_exists).

annotations.label: Burali-Forti paradox

anchor.snippet: is an ordinal} led, in the year 1897, to the Burali-Forti paradox

annotations.note: The conclusion of Burali–Forti — that the ordinals form a proper class — is captured by ZFSet.isOrdinal_notMem_univ (verified); the paradox itself is not named.

annotations.label: Cantor's paradox

anchor.snippet: is a cardinal} produced Cantor's paradox in 1897

annotations.note: Cantor's theorem `a < 2^a` behind the paradox is Cardinal.cantor (verified); the naive-set paradox itself is not stated.

annotations.label: Russell's paradox (formula)

anchor.snippet: the set of all sets that do not contain themselves as elements, gave Russell's paradox in 1902

annotations.note: The Russell class of non-self-membered sets isn't constructed, but `Class.univ_notMem_univ` and `ZFSet.mem_irrefl` (verified) show the paradoxical set cannot exist.

annotations.label: No universe

anchor.snippet: There is no universe

annotations.note: That the universal class is not itself a set is formalized as Class.univ_notMem_univ (verified via decl_exists).

annotations.label: Curry's paradox

anchor.snippet: This is Curry's paradox

annotations.note: Curry's paradox has no counterpart declaration in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Axiom of regularity

anchor.snippet: by demanding the axiom of regularity to hold

annotations.note: ZFSet.regularity states every non-empty ZFC set has an ∈-minimal element (verified via decl_exists).

annotations.label: No set is element of itself

anchor.snippet: no set is an element of itself

annotations.note: ZFSet.mem_irrefl: `∀ (x : ZFSet), x ∉ x` (verified via decl_exists).
